programs.name: Medicaid Buy-In for Children (MBIC) / Qualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB), Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary (SLMB), Qualifying Individual (QI)
programs.tagline: Health coverage for disabled children and Medicare cost help for low-income seniors in Texas.

localEntities.name: Local HHSC offices statewide; call 2-1-1 for nearest

programs.intro:
If your disabled child needs health coverage or your parent is 65+ struggling with Medicare costs, Texas offers several programs that can help. For children under 18 with disabilities, Medicaid Buy-In for Children (MBIC) provides full health coverage for families earning up to 300% of the federal poverty level — about $66,700/year for a family of four. For elderly parents on Medicare, three programs help with premiums and cost-sharing: QMB covers all Medicare costs for those with income under $1,275/month (individual), SLMB pays Medicare Part B premiums for income up to $1,526/month, and QI also covers Part B premiums for income up to $1,718/month but has limited funding.

Unlike traditional Medicaid, MBIC requires a monthly premium based on your family's income and size, typically $20-40/month. Because these programs have different eligibility rules and benefits, understanding which one fits your family's situation can save thousands in medical costs annually.
programs.savingsSource: Free service - Medicare premium and cost-sharing coverage

summary: Income limits vary by program tier
structuredEligibility.ageRequirement: Under 18 for MBIC, 65+ for Medicare programs
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Child must meet SSI disability criteria (condition significantly restricts daily activities for 12+ months)
otherRequirements: Texas resident
U.S. citizen or legal resident
Child must be unmarried for MBIC
Medicare Part A enrollment required for QMB/SLMB/QI
structuredEligibility.povertyLevelReference: 300% FPL for MBIC, 100-135% FPL for Medicare programs

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at YourTexasBenefits.com or call 2-1-1 — processing time varies by office.

steps.title: Download the correct form
steps.description: MBIC families use Form H1200-MBIC. Medicare programs use Form H1200 or apply online at YourTexasBenefits.com.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of income, disability determination (for MBIC), Medicare card, Social Security numbers, and bank statements.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Mail completed form to HHSC, apply online, or call 2-1-1 for help submitting.

steps.title: Confirm receipt
steps.description: Call 2-1-1 with the head of household's Social Security number to verify your application was received.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified - varies by local HHSC office
applicationGuide.waitlist: QI program has limited funding and may have waitlists
applicationGuide.tip: For MBIC, confirm your child meets SSI-level disability criteria before applying - not all disabilities qualify.

tiers.name: MBIC (Children)
tiers.description: Full Medicaid coverage for disabled children
tiers.coverage: All medical, dental, vision, therapies, prescriptions. Monthly premium required.

tiers.name: QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.description: Full Medicare cost coverage for lowest-income seniors
tiers.coverage: Medicare Part A & B premiums, deductibles, copays

tiers.name: QI (Qualifying Individual)
tiers.description: Limited funding Medicare Part B premium help
tiers.coverage: Medicare Part B premium only (subject to waitlist)

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Social Security numbers for all family members

contentSections.text: QI program has limited annual funding and may have waitlists. Apply early in the year for best chances of enrollment.

MBIC requires a monthly premium (typically $20-40) based on your family income and size. It's not free, but provides comprehensive coverage for disabled children whose families earn too much for regular Medicaid.

faqs.question: My child has autism - do they automatically qualify for MBIC?
faqs.answer: No. Your child must meet SSI-level disability criteria, meaning their condition significantly restricts daily activities for 12+ months. Having a diagnosis alone doesn't guarantee eligibility - the disability must meet Social Security's specific functional requirements.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify for QMB?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your parent's primary home doesn't count toward the $9,660 asset limit for individuals or $14,470 for couples. There's no home equity cap specified in Texas.

faqs.question: What if my family income is just over 300% of the poverty level for MBIC?
faqs.answer: You won't qualify for MBIC, but your disabled child may be eligible for other programs. Call 2-1-1 to discuss alternatives - there may be other Medicaid pathways or state programs available.

faqs.question: If Mom gets QMB, can doctors still bill her for Medicare costs?
faqs.answer: No. Once your parent has QMB, providers cannot bill them for Medicare deductibles, copays, or coinsurance. The provider must accept what Medicare and Medicaid pay - your parent pays nothing.

faqs.question: How much will we pay monthly for MBIC coverage?
faqs.answer: MBIC premiums are based on your adjusted gross income, family size, and whether you have employer insurance. Typical premiums range from $20-40/month, but the exact amount depends on your specific situation.

faqs.question: What's the difference between SLMB and QI if they both cover Part B premiums?
faqs.answer: SLMB has guaranteed funding if you qualify, while QI has limited annual funding and may have waitlists. QI also has slightly higher income limits - up to $1,718/month for individuals versus $1,526/month for SLMB.

programs.name: STAR+PLUS
programs.tagline: If your parent needs daily care help but wants to stay home, this Texas Medicaid program covers all medical costs plus home services — but has a years-long waitlist.

localEntities.name: MCOs: Community First Health Plans, Molina Healthcare, Superior HealthPlan, Wellpoint/Anthem; local HHS offices via 211

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and needs help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management, STAR+PLUS covers all their medical care plus home services to help them avoid nursing home placement. This Texas Medicaid program provides complete healthcare (doctor visits, hospital, prescriptions, dental, vision) plus personal care attendants, adult day programs, home modifications, and specialized equipment at no cost to qualifying seniors.

Your parent must have income below $2,901/month and assets under $2,000, plus meet nursing home level of care requirements. Because this program has years-long waitlists for home services, you need to join the interest list first even if your parent doesn't need help today. Those already in nursing facilities can get services immediately.

exemptAssets: Life insurance up to $1,500 face value

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need nursing home level of care (requires daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other activities of daily living, determined by physician assessment)
otherRequirements: Texas resident
Not enrolled in another Medicaid waiver program

applicationGuide.summary: First join the interest list by calling 211, then apply for Medicaid online when invited (processing time varies).

steps.title: Join the Interest List
steps.description: Call 211 or 877-541-7905 to get on the STAR+PLUS interest list. This is required even if your parent doesn't need services immediately.

steps.title: Get Physician Assessment
steps.description: Have your parent's doctor complete the Nursing Facility Level of Care assessment to document their need for daily care assistance.

steps.title: Apply for Medicaid When Invited
steps.description: When called from the interest list, apply at yourtexasbenefits.com, call 211, or visit your local HHS office with required documents.

steps.title: Choose Your Health Plan
steps.description: Select from available MCOs in your area (Community First, Molina, Superior, or Wellpoint) as benefits and extras vary by plan.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Medicaid approval varies; HCBS assessment by MCO post-Medicaid approval
applicationGuide.waitlist: Years-long wait for home and community services; nursing facility residents can get services immediately
applicationGuide.tip: Join the interest list now even if your parent doesn't need services today — the waitlist is years long and you can't get services without being on it first.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits
contentSections.footnote: 2025 limits. Each spouse evaluated individually up to this amount regardless of marital status.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts Toward $2,000
excluded: Primary home (if equity ≤ $730,000 and they live there or intend to return)
Life insurance with face value up to $1,500

tiers.name: Basic Medicaid Services
tiers.coverage: Doctor visits, hospital care, prescriptions, dental, vision, medical transport

tiers.name: Home & Community Services
tiers.description: Requires invitation from interest list (years-long wait)
tiers.incomeLimit: Under $2,901/month + nursing home level of care
tiers.coverage: Personal care attendants, adult day programs (up to 5 days/week), home modifications, specialized equipment, respite care

categories.name: Financial
items: SSI or Social Security award letters

categories.name: Medical
items: Medical records showing need for daily care
Physician certification for nursing facility level of care

contentSections.text: The waitlist for home services is years long in most Texas counties. Join the interest list now even if your parent doesn't need help today — you cannot access home services without being on the list first.
Choose your health plan (MCO) carefully — each offers different extra benefits like Weight Watchers subscriptions, camp allowances, or specialized supplies, and availability varies by region.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if it's worth more than $2,000?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home doesn't count toward the $2,000 asset limit as long as your parent lives there (or intends to return) and the home equity is $730,000 or less.

faqs.question: My parent makes $2,950/month — just over the limit. Are there any options?
faqs.answer: No, the income limit is firm at $2,901/month for individuals in 2025. Even spouses are evaluated individually at this same limit. Your parent would need to spend down income to qualify.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist for home services really?
faqs.answer: Wait times vary by region but are typically several years for home and community services. However, if your parent is already in a nursing facility, they can access services immediately and potentially transition home.

faqs.question: What's the difference between the health plans I have to choose from?
faqs.answer: Community First, Molina, Superior, and Wellpoint all provide the same basic services, but offer different extras (like Weight Watchers subscriptions or specialized supplies) and serve different regions. Ask about plan-specific benefits in your area.

faqs.question: Does my parent have to pay anything if they qualify?
faqs.answer: Medical services are free. However, if your parent enters a nursing facility, they must contribute their income toward the cost of care, keeping only a small personal allowance.

faqs.question: Can my parent get services while we wait on the home services list?
faqs.answer: Yes, once approved for STAR+PLUS Medicaid, your parent gets immediate access to all medical services (doctor visits, hospital, prescriptions, dental, vision). Only the home and community services (personal care, adult day programs) have the waitlist.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay home, PACE provides all medical care, transportation, and support services through one coordinated team.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and has been certified as needing nursing home-level care, PACE allows them to stay in their home while receiving comprehensive medical and social services. Because PACE coordinates all care under one program, your parent won't need to juggle multiple doctors, insurance plans, or service providers — one team manages everything from primary care to prescription drugs to transportation.

PACE covers all medical costs, hospital stays, prescription drugs, adult day center care, home care services, and transportation with no copays for participants who qualify for both Medicare and Medicaid. Your parent must live in a PACE service area (currently only available in El Paso area through Bienvivir) and be certified by Texas as needing nursing home-level care while still being able to live safely in the community.
programs.savingsSource: Free comprehensive medical and social services

summary: Texas-certified for nursing home level of care
Not enrolled in Medicare Advantage or hospice
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be state-certified as needing nursing home level of care (a clinical assessment of whether your parent needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility)
otherRequirements: Live in PACE provider service area
Able to live safely in community with PACE support
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage, Medicare prepayment plan, Medicare prescription drug plan, or hospice

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local PACE provider directly for intake — they guide you through the entire enrollment process including state certification.

steps.title: Contact PACE Provider
steps.description: Call Bienvivir at (915) 562-3444 ext. 1442 if in El Paso area, or complete their online eligibility form

steps.title: Initial Assessment
steps.description: PACE provider evaluates if your parent meets basic requirements and lives in service area

steps.title: State Certification
steps.description: Texas conducts clinical assessment to certify your parent needs nursing home level of care

steps.title: Enrollment Approval
steps.description: State approves enrollment after provider assistance with paperwork and assessments

applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible waitlist depending on provider capacity
applicationGuide.tip: PACE providers handle most of the paperwork for you — they're motivated to help because enrollment benefits their program too.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts (For Medicaid Eligibility)
excluded: Burial plots and funeral funds up to $1,500

tiers.name: Medicare + Medicaid (Most Common)
tiers.coverage: All services covered with no copays, deductibles, or limits

tiers.name: Medicare Only
tiers.coverage: Medical services covered; may have costs for social services

tiers.name: Private Pay
tiers.coverage: Full cost responsibility for comprehensive services

categories.name: Identity & Residence
items: Government-issued photo ID or birth certificate
Proof of address in PACE service area

categories.name: Medical
items: Recent medical records and doctor visits

contentSections.text: PACE is only available in limited areas of Texas. Currently, Bienvivir serves the El Paso region. If your parent lives outside this area, they cannot enroll in PACE.
Your parent cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage and PACE at the same time. If they have Medicare Advantage, they'll need to disenroll during open enrollment or with a special enrollment period.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for PACE?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE is designed to help people stay in their homes. Her house doesn't count toward asset limits, and PACE provides home care services to help her live safely in the community.

faqs.question: What if Dad has Medicare Advantage — can he still get PACE?
faqs.answer: No, he cannot be enrolled in both. He would need to disenroll from Medicare Advantage first, which he can do during open enrollment (October 15 - December 7) or if he qualifies for a special enrollment period.

faqs.question: How much will PACE cost if Mom doesn't qualify for Medicaid?
faqs.answer: If she has Medicare only, Medicare will cover medical services but she may have costs for social services. If she doesn't qualify for either, she can pay privately, but costs aren't specified — contact the PACE provider directly for pricing.

faqs.question: What does 'nursing home level of care' actually mean?
faqs.answer: It means your parent needs substantial daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, mobility, or medication management. A state assessor will evaluate whether your parent meets this clinical standard through medical records and in-person assessment.

faqs.question: Can we choose which doctors and services Dad uses through PACE?
faqs.answer: PACE uses its own network of providers and care team. Your dad would need to switch to PACE doctors and services, but the benefit is that one coordinated team manages all his care instead of juggling multiple providers.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is just over the Medicaid limit?
faqs.answer: She can still enroll in PACE and may qualify for Medicaid through spend-down (paying medical expenses to reduce countable income) or other pathways. The PACE provider will help explore options to make her eligible for dual coverage.

programs.name: Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)
programs.tagline: Get $100–298/month toward groceries if your parent is 60+ and on a fixed income.

localEntities.name: Local HHSC offices (locate via 211 or YourTexasBenefits.com)

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and struggling with grocery costs, SNAP can provide $100–298/month on an EBT card for food purchases. Because your parent is a senior, they only need to meet the net income test (not gross income) — meaning medical expenses over $35/month and high shelter costs are deducted from their income to help them qualify.

Seniors in Texas average about $188/month in SNAP benefits. The exact amount depends on household size and net income after deductions. Your parent can use the EBT card at most grocery stores for food items, but not for hot prepared foods, alcohol, or tobacco.

Texas requires an asset test ($2,000 limit for one person), but your parent's home, most vehicles, and household goods don't count toward this limit. Social Security and pension income do count, but medical expenses and shelter costs above certain amounts are deducted before determining eligibility.
programs.savingsSource: Based on typical senior benefits of $100–298/month

otherRequirements: Must live in Texas
Include all household members who share food
Seniors 60+ exempt from work requirements

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at YourTexasBenefits.com — typically takes 30 days to process, or 7 days if expedited.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect ID, income proof (Social Security award letters, pension statements), bank statements, rent/mortgage records, and medical expense receipts over $35/month.

steps.title: Apply online or request paper application
steps.description: Go to YourTexasBenefits.com to apply online, or call 211 to request a mailed application.

steps.title: Submit application and documents
steps.description: Complete the application with all household members who share food, even if they're not eligible for benefits themselves.

steps.title: Attend interview if required
steps.description: HHSC may schedule a phone or in-person interview to verify information and calculate net income after deductions.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Typically 30 days; expedited (7 days) if income <$150/month and assets <$100, or utility shutoff risk
applicationGuide.tip: Medical expenses over $35/month and high shelter costs are deducted from income — gather these receipts to maximize your parent's chances of qualifying.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Life insurance cash value (varies by policy)

contentSections.text: Seniors only need to meet the net income test — medical expenses over $35/month and high shelter costs are deducted from income, so your parent may qualify even if their Social Security seems too high.

faqs.question: Can my parent qualify if they own their home and have some savings?
faqs.answer: Yes, likely. The home doesn't count toward the $2,000 asset limit, and most vehicles are also exempt. Only liquid assets like bank accounts, stocks, and accessible retirement funds count toward the limit.

faqs.question: My parent's Social Security is $1,400/month. Are they automatically disqualified?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Because your parent is 60+, medical expenses over $35/month and high shelter costs are deducted from their income. If they pay $200+ in medical expenses and rent/utilities, their net income could fall below the $1,255/month limit.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives with me but we buy groceries separately?
faqs.answer: If your parent buys and prepares food separately, they can apply as a one-person household. But if you share groceries or meals, the entire household must be included in the application, which could affect eligibility.

faqs.question: How much will my parent actually receive in SNAP benefits?
faqs.answer: It depends on their net income after deductions. The benefit equals the maximum allotment ($298 for one person) minus 30% of their net income. Texas seniors average about $188/month.

faqs.question: Can SNAP benefits be used for groceries delivered to my parent's home?
faqs.answer: Yes, many grocery stores and services like Amazon Fresh accept EBT cards for eligible food items. The card works for any food items that would be allowed in-store — no hot prepared foods, alcohol, or tobacco.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income or expenses change after approval?
faqs.answer: Report changes within 10 days to your local HHSC office. Increases in medical expenses or rent could actually increase benefits, while income increases might reduce them. Benefits are reviewed annually.

programs.name: Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.tagline: Get help paying utility bills with one-time payments up to $12,300 sent directly to your parent's energy company.

localEntities.name: Local CAAs via 211Texas.org or (877) 399-8939; full subrecipient list on TDHCA site

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to pay heating or cooling bills on a fixed income, LIHEAP can provide one-time payments sent directly to their utility company. Because the program covers both regular assistance ($1 to $12,300) and crisis help (up to $2,400 extra for shutoff notices), your parent can get help whether they're behind on bills or facing an emergency.

Income limit: $2,815/month for a single person or $3,681/month for a couple (2025). No asset limits — your parent can own a home, have savings, and still qualify. The program prioritizes seniors, disabled individuals, and families facing shutoffs, but anyone meeting income requirements can apply.
programs.savingsSource: One-time payment ranges from Texas CEAP program data

summary: Income at or below 150% federal poverty level
otherRequirements: Texas resident with proof of address
U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen (lawful permanent resident, refugee)
Recent utility bill in applicant's name

applicationGuide.summary: Call 211 to find your local office — you'll be placed on a waitlist and called when funding is available.

steps.title: Find your local Community Action Agency
steps.description: Call 211 or (877) 399-8939, or visit 211Texas.org to locate the office serving your parent's county. Each of Texas's 254 counties has a local agency.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of income, Texas ID, recent utility bill in your parent's name, and proof of residency like a lease or utility bill.

steps.title: Submit application to local office
steps.description: Apply by phone, mail, or in-person at your local Community Action Agency. Each office has its own application form.

steps.title: Get placed on waitlist
steps.description: Your parent will be added to the local waitlist. A caseworker will call when their turn comes up and funding is available.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Varies by region and waitlist length
applicationGuide.waitlist: Yes, common. Applications are placed in line by local site and depend on federal funding availability.
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent has an online utility account, print recent statements before applying — this speeds up the documentation process.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size
contentSections.footnote: 2025 limits at 150% federal poverty level. Gross monthly household income before taxes.

categories.name: Identity
items: Texas driver's license or state ID

categories.name: Financial
items: Recent utility bill in applicant's name

categories.name: Residence
items: Any official mail showing Texas address

contentSections.text: Funding is limited and waitlists are common. Even if your parent qualifies, they may wait weeks or months for assistance depending on their local area's funding.
Payments go directly to the utility company, not to your parent. This means past-due bills get paid automatically without your parent having to manage the transaction.

faqs.question: What if Mom gets an automatic qualification benefit like SNAP or SSI?
faqs.answer: She automatically meets the income requirement and doesn't need to provide additional income documentation. The local office will still verify her other eligibility requirements.

faqs.question: Can Dad apply if he's behind on his electric bill and got a shutoff notice?
faqs.answer: Yes, and he'll get priority processing. Crisis assistance provides up to $2,400 extra on top of regular assistance, specifically for households facing shutoffs or energy emergencies.

faqs.question: Does Mom's house or savings count against her eligibility?
faqs.answer: No. LIHEAP has no asset limits. Your parent can own their home, have savings accounts, and still qualify as long as their monthly income is below the limit.

faqs.question: What if Mom lives with other family members who aren't on the utility bill?
faqs.answer: Only people sharing the utility bill count as the household for income purposes. This is different from SNAP — household size is based on who shares energy costs, not who lives at the address.

faqs.question: How often can Dad get LIHEAP assistance?
faqs.answer: Once per federal program year (typically October to September). However, he can receive both regular assistance and crisis assistance in the same year if he faces an energy emergency.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over the monthly limit?
faqs.answer: The income limits are firm — there's no flexibility for being slightly over. However, if his income drops in future months, he can reapply during the next program year.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.tagline: Free home energy improvements that can cut your parent's heating and cooling bills by hundreds each year.

localEntities.name: Examples: TCOG (Sherman), SPCAA (South Plains), ATCog, Dallas County HHS, Austin Energy, CCSCT (Uvalde region counties), Oncor low-income weatherization[9].

programs.intro:
If your parent's household income is below 200% of the federal poverty level, they can get free home energy improvements through the Weatherization Assistance Program. This includes adding insulation, sealing air leaks, upgrading heating and cooling systems, and other weatherization services that reduce monthly utility bills.

Your parent must live in the home as their primary residence. The program prioritizes elderly households, people with disabilities, and families with children under 6, so your parent may move up the waitlist faster. Because each home gets a professional energy audit first, the specific improvements depend on what your parent's house actually needs — not every home gets the same services.

You cannot apply directly to the state. Each region of Texas has its own local agency that handles applications, so you'll need to find your parent's specific area provider first.

summary: Income at or below 200% of federal poverty level
Live in home as primary residence
Single-family home, duplex, triplex, or fourplex
otherRequirements: U.S. Citizen, U.S. National, or Qualified Alien status for all household members
Home must be single-family, duplex, triplex, or fourplex (mobile homes may be excluded in some areas)
Home must pass energy audit to confirm weatherization is needed
Cannot have been weatherized in the last 10 years in some regions

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local regional agency by phone or mail — processing time varies widely by region and funding availability.

steps.title: Find your regional agency
steps.description: Look up your county's weatherization provider on the TDHCA website or call the examples listed for your area.

steps.title: Gather 30-day income proof
steps.description: Collect pay stubs, Social Security award letters, or other income documentation for the last 30 days only — no bank statements or tax returns.

steps.title: Complete agency-specific application
steps.description: Each regional provider has their own forms and requirements — some accept online applications, others require mail or in-person visits.

steps.title: Schedule energy audit
steps.description: If income-qualified, a technician will assess your parent's home to determine what weatherization measures are needed.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Varies widely by region, number of clients, and funding availability
applicationGuide.waitlist: Common due to funding limits; elderly and disabled households often receive priority
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent rents, get written landlord permission before applying — it's required for all rental properties.

categories.name: Identity
items: Driver's license, state ID, passport, or military ID for all household members 18+
Birth certificate, naturalization certificate, or U.S. passport for all household members to prove citizenship

categories.name: Income (Last 30 Days Only)
items: Pay stubs for all working household members 18+
Social Security, SSI, or SSDI award letters
Unemployment, pension, or workers compensation documentation
Notarized Declaration of Income Statement if anyone has no income

categories.name: Housing
items: Current electric and gas bills with 12-month history
Landlord Permission Form if your parent rents the home

contentSections.text: Waitlists are common due to limited funding. Even if your parent qualifies, they may wait months or longer for services depending on their region and priority level.

Income documentation must be from the last 30 days only — the agency will not accept bank statements, W-2s, or tax returns. If your parent had no income in the last 30 days, they'll need a notarized zero-income declaration.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is slightly over the 200% poverty level limit?
faqs.answer: They won't qualify for this program, but some utility companies offer their own weatherization programs with higher income limits. Contact your parent's electric or gas company directly to ask about customer assistance programs.

faqs.question: Can my parent get weatherization help if they rent their home?
faqs.answer: Yes, but the landlord must give written permission before any work can be done. Some landlords may also need to commit to not raising rent or evicting tenants for a certain period after the improvements.

faqs.question: What if the energy audit shows my parent's home doesn't need weatherization?
faqs.answer: No services will be provided. The program only weatherizes homes that pass the energy audit and show they would benefit from specific improvements that meet federal energy-savings requirements.

faqs.question: Does this program replace broken furnaces or air conditioners?
faqs.answer: It typically includes tune-ups and repairs of heating and cooling systems, but full HVAC system replacements are not common. The specific services depend on what the energy audit determines will be most cost-effective for your parent's home.

faqs.question: Why can't I apply directly to the state of Texas?
faqs.answer: Texas contracts with local agencies in each region to run the program. You must apply through your parent's specific regional provider — they each have different applications, requirements, and waitlists.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives in a mobile home?
faqs.answer: Mobile homes are eligible in some regions but excluded in others. Contact your regional provider to confirm — eligibility rules for manufactured homes vary significantly across Texas.

programs.name: Texas State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)
programs.tagline: Free one-on-one help choosing Medicare plans and applying for cost-saving programs.

localEntities.name: Local SHIP sites via statewide network; contact 1-800-252-9240 for nearest

programs.intro:
If your parent is overwhelmed by Medicare choices or paying too much for coverage, Texas SHIP provides free one-on-one counseling to help them find the right plan and savings. Trained counselors review your parent's current Medicare Parts A, B, C, and D coverage, compare costs across plans, and help apply for programs that can lower premiums and prescription costs.

Because SHIP counselors are independent and don't sell insurance, they can give unbiased advice tailored to your parent's specific health needs and budget. The service is available to anyone with Medicare — no income limits or fees. In Texas, this program is also called the Health Information, Counseling, and Advocacy Program (HICAP).

otherRequirements: Must be Medicare-eligible (typically age 65+ or under 65 with certain disabilities)

applicationGuide.summary: Call to schedule a counseling session — typically available immediately or by appointment.

steps.title: Call the Texas SHIP hotline
steps.description: Call 1-800-252-9240 to request counseling and find your nearest local office.

steps.title: Schedule your session
steps.description: Set up in-person or phone counseling based on your preference and availability.

steps.title: Gather Medicare information
steps.description: Have your parent's Medicare card and current plan details ready for the session.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Counseling provided upon contact, typically immediate or by appointment
applicationGuide.tip: Call during off-peak times (not October-December Open Enrollment) for faster scheduling.

offices.name: Statewide Hotline
offices.address: Call for nearest local counseling site

contentSections.text: SHIP provides counseling only — they don't pay for healthcare or provide direct financial aid. But they can help your parent apply for programs that do, like Medicare Savings Programs and Extra Help for prescriptions.

faqs.question: What's the difference between SHIP and the Medicare.gov website?
faqs.answer: SHIP counselors provide personalized, one-on-one help specific to your parent's situation. Because they don't sell insurance, they can give unbiased recommendations and help navigate complex scenarios that generic websites can't address.

faqs.question: Can SHIP help if my parent already has Medicare Advantage or a supplement plan?
faqs.answer: Yes. SHIP counselors review existing coverage to see if your parent could save money or get better benefits by switching plans during Open Enrollment (October 15 - December 7).

faqs.question: Will SHIP help us apply for programs to lower Medicare costs?
faqs.answer: Yes. SHIP counselors can help your parent apply for Medicare Savings Programs (which pay Medicare premiums) and Extra Help (which reduces prescription drug costs). They'll determine eligibility and assist with applications.

faqs.question: How long does a SHIP counseling session take?
faqs.answer: Sessions typically last 30-60 minutes, depending on your parent's needs. Complex situations involving multiple plan comparisons or benefit applications may take longer.

faqs.question: Is there a catch since the service is free?
faqs.answer: No catch. SHIP is funded by federal and state grants specifically to provide unbiased Medicare counseling. Counselors cannot sell insurance or receive commissions, ensuring their advice serves your parent's best interests.

faqs.question: What if we need help during Medicare Open Enrollment?
faqs.answer: SHIP is busiest during Open Enrollment (October 15 - December 7), so call early in the period or consider scheduling before October. Some areas may have higher demand during this time.

programs.name: Meals on Wheels
programs.tagline: If your parent is homebound and struggles to prepare meals, they can get hot, nutritious meals delivered to their door — no income limits.

localEntities.name: Meals on Wheels North Central Texas (Johnson/Ellis/Navarro)
Meals on Wheels of Tarrant County
Meals on Wheels Senior Services of Rockwall County

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and homebound — meaning they can't easily leave home or prepare nutritious meals without help — Meals on Wheels delivers hot meals directly to their door. Because this isn't one statewide program, you'll need to contact the specific local provider in your parent's area. Most programs deliver one hot meal per weekday, with some offering weekend frozen meals.

Because volunteers deliver meals during a specific window (typically 10:30 a.m. to 1:00 p.m.), your parent gets both nutrition and a daily safety check. No income limits apply — eligibility is based on need, not finances. Voluntary contributions are requested but no one is denied for inability to pay.

The main requirement is that your parent must be primarily homebound and unable to prepare nutritious meals consistently. Priority goes to those with the greatest need — severe disabilities, dementia, or limited social support.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be primarily homebound and unable to prepare nutritious meals without consistent daytime assistance
otherRequirements: Must live in the local program's delivery zone
Must be able to accept meals during delivery window (typically 10:30 a.m.-1:00 p.m.)
Priority given to those with greatest economic/social need, severe disabilities, or dementia

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local Meals on Wheels provider directly — anyone can make a referral, and a caseworker will visit within days to assess eligibility.

steps.title: Find your local provider
steps.description: Use the county directory below to find the Meals on Wheels program serving your parent's area. Each county or region has its own independent provider.

steps.title: Call or submit online referral
steps.description: Contact the local provider by phone or fill out their online referral form. You, your parent, friends, or even hospital staff can make the referral.

steps.title: Schedule home assessment
steps.description: A case manager or social worker will visit your parent's home to assess their needs, living situation, and meal preparation abilities.

steps.title: Begin meal delivery
steps.description: If approved, meals typically begin within a week, though some areas may have waitlists that extend processing time.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Varies by location — some programs start within a week, others have waitlists. Caseworker visits typically scheduled within 2 working days.
applicationGuide.waitlist: Some programs have waitlists that may delay service start
applicationGuide.tip: Have your parent's doctor information, dietary restrictions, and emergency contacts ready when you call — this speeds up the referral process.

offices.name: Meals on Wheels of Tarrant County
Meals on Wheels Senior Services of Rockwall County
Meals on Wheels North Central Texas

categories.name: Basic Information
items: Proof of age (driver's license, birth certificate)
Proof of residence in service area

categories.name: Medical Information
items: List of dietary restrictions or special needs
Description of health problems affecting meal preparation

categories.name: Emergency Contacts
items: Family member or friend contact information

contentSections.text: This is not one statewide program. You must contact the specific local provider in your parent's county or service area. If your parent can easily leave home or has regular cooking help, they may not qualify.
Short-term meal delivery is available for recovery periods (typically up to 6 weeks) if your parent is temporarily unable to cook after surgery or hospitalization.

faqs.question: What if my parent owns a car but can't drive anymore?
faqs.answer: Car ownership alone may disqualify your parent from some programs. However, if they can't actually drive or leave home safely due to physical or cognitive issues, they may still qualify. The home assessment will determine if they're functionally homebound.

faqs.question: Can my parent get meals if they live in assisted living?
faqs.answer: This varies by local provider and the level of meal preparation help already provided. If your parent lives independently but struggles with cooking, they may qualify. The caseworker will assess whether additional meal support is needed.

faqs.question: What if my parent can't afford to contribute anything?
faqs.answer: Your parent will still receive meals. Voluntary contributions are requested but no one is denied for inability to pay. The program is designed to serve those who need it regardless of their financial situation.

faqs.question: How long can my parent receive meals?
faqs.answer: There's typically no time limit for ongoing service if your parent continues to meet eligibility requirements. However, short-term service during recovery periods is usually limited to about 6 weeks.

faqs.question: What if no local provider serves my parent's area?
faqs.answer: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging for alternatives. Not all Texas counties have Meals on Wheels programs, but there may be other senior nutrition programs or meal delivery services available.

faqs.question: Can meals be customized for my parent's diabetes or other conditions?
faqs.answer: Many programs offer medically-tailored meals for conditions like diabetes, heart disease, or renal diets. Mention any dietary restrictions or medical conditions during the referral — this information helps them prepare appropriate meals.

programs.name: Community Based Alternatives (CBA) Waiver
programs.tagline: If your parent needs nursing home level care but wants to stay home, this Texas Medicaid waiver covers home care services and supports.
programs.intro:
If your parent qualifies for nursing home care but wants to stay in their own home, the Community Based Alternatives (CBA) Waiver can pay for home care services instead. This Texas Medicaid program covers personal care attendants, respite care, home modifications, medical equipment, and nursing services — all designed to keep your parent safely at home.

Your parent must be 21 or older, qualify for Medicaid, and meet the clinical criteria for nursing home level of care (needing daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, or medication management). Because this waiver has limited funding slots, there are waitlists in most areas — sometimes lasting years. Income limit: approximately $2,829/month for a single person (2025). Asset limit: $2,000 for an individual, $3,000 for a couple.

Because the waiver caps total services at what a nursing home would cost, your parent gets a customized care plan within that budget. One care coordinator manages all services, so you won't need to juggle multiple providers.

summary: Qualifies for nursing home level care
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet nursing facility level of care (clinical assessment showing need for daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, medication management, or behavioral issues requiring 24-hour supervision)
otherRequirements: Must qualify for Medicaid
Must choose waiver services over nursing facility care
Service plan cost cannot exceed nursing facility payment rate

applicationGuide.summary: Apply for Medicaid first, then join the waiver interest list — processing takes 45 days for Medicaid, but waitlists can be years long.

steps.title: Apply for Medicaid
steps.description: Submit Form H1200 online at yourtexasbenefits.com or call 2-1-1. You must qualify for Medicaid before getting waiver services.

steps.title: Request clinical assessment
steps.description: Ask your doctor to document that your parent needs nursing home level care. This clinical assessment is required for waiver eligibility.

steps.title: Join the interest list
steps.description: Contact local STAR+PLUS managed care organizations or providers to get on the waiver interest list. Do this even if you're not ready for services yet.

steps.title: Wait for slot availability
steps.description: When a waiver slot opens, the managed care organization will contact you to develop a service plan and coordinate care.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Medicaid eligibility determination typically 45 days, but waiver interest list wait times vary by region
applicationGuide.waitlist: Yes, interest/waiting lists common for HCBS waivers as not an entitlement; length varies by region and priority (e.g., highest need first)
applicationGuide.tip: Join the interest list immediately — even if your parent doesn't need services now. Waitlists can be years long.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Cash value of life insurance over $1,500

categories.name: Medical
items: Physician statement documenting need for nursing home level care

contentSections.text: Waitlists can be years long in high-demand areas like Houston and Dallas. Apply to the interest list as soon as your parent might need services, even if not immediately.
Your parent cannot be enrolled in both the waiver and a nursing facility at the same time. They must make an informed choice between home-based services and facility care.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for the CBA waiver?
faqs.answer: Yes, as long as the house equity is under $730,000. The home is exempt from asset limits if your parent lives there or intends to return, or if a spouse or disabled child lives there.

faqs.question: What if her income is just over $2,829 per month?
faqs.answer: She may still qualify through a Medicaid spend-down process, where excess income goes toward medical expenses. A caseworker can calculate if this makes financial sense for your family.

faqs.question: How long are the waitlists really?
faqs.answer: Wait times vary dramatically by region and your parent's priority level. Urban areas like Houston and Dallas typically have longer waits (sometimes years), while rural areas may have shorter waits but fewer service providers.

faqs.question: What happens if the services cost more than a nursing home?
faqs.answer: They can't. The waiver caps your parent's service plan at what Texas would pay for nursing home care. If needs exceed that cost, the waiver isn't the right fit.

faqs.question: Can we choose which services Mom gets?
faqs.answer: Yes, within the budget limit. A care coordinator will work with you to create a customized service plan based on your parent's specific needs and preferences.

faqs.question: What if Mom needs more care while on the waitlist?
faqs.answer: She can still receive some home care through regular Medicaid or private pay while waiting for the waiver. The managed care organization may also provide limited services to people on the interest list.

programs.name: Community Caregiver Support Program
programs.tagline: Get help managing care for your aging parent through local support services and resources in Texas.

localEntities.name: Local Aging and Disability Resource Centers (ADRCs)

programs.intro:
If you're caring for an aging parent in Texas, you may qualify for support services that help manage their daily care needs. The Community Caregiver Support Program connects families to local resources and non-medical assistance for older adults who need help with everyday tasks like bathing, dressing, or meal preparation.

This program is delivered through local Aging and Disability Resource Centers (ADRCs) across Texas. Your parent must be a Texas resident and demonstrate they need help with daily activities to remain living independently. The specific services available depend on your local area, but typically include assistance with personal care, household tasks, and connecting you to other community resources.

Note: Our research found limited official documentation for a program with this exact name. You may be referred to related programs like Community Care for Aged/Disabled (CCAD) or STAR+PLUS when you call.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management to live independently
otherRequirements: Must be Texas resident
Cannot be receiving Medicaid for the same services

applicationGuide.summary: Call your local Aging and Disability Resource Center at 1-855-937-2372 to start the application process.

steps.title: Call the statewide ADRC number
steps.description: Dial 1-855-937-2372 to be connected to your local Aging and Disability Resource Center

steps.title: Explain your parent's care needs
steps.description: Describe what daily activities your parent needs help with and their current living situation

steps.title: Schedule an assessment
steps.description: Your local ADRC will arrange to evaluate your parent's eligibility and care needs

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Bring proof of income, Texas residency, and any medical documentation of care needs

applicationGuide.tip: Wait times and available services may vary by region, so ask about alternatives if there's a waiting list in your area.

categories.name: Identity
items: Texas driver's license or state ID

categories.name: Financial
items: Proof of income (Social Security statements, pension statements)
Proof of Texas residency (utility bills, lease agreement)

categories.name: Medical
items: Documentation of care needs from doctor or healthcare provider

contentSections.text: The program name 'Community Caregiver Support Program' may refer to different services depending on your county. Be prepared to be referred to Community Care for Aged/Disabled (CCAD) or STAR+PLUS programs, which provide similar caregiver support services.

faqs.question: What if I can't find a program with this exact name when I call?
faqs.answer: You may be referred to related programs like Community Care for Aged/Disabled (CCAD) or STAR+PLUS. These provide similar caregiver support services through the same local ADRCs.

faqs.question: Can my parent get these services if they already have Medicaid?
faqs.answer: They cannot receive duplicate services. If Medicaid already covers certain care needs, this program won't provide the same services, but may offer additional support not covered by Medicaid.

faqs.question: What's the difference between counties in Texas?
faqs.answer: Each area has its own ADRC that administers services locally. Available providers and wait times may vary by region, but eligibility requirements remain consistent statewide.

faqs.question: How long does it take to get services once approved?
faqs.answer: Processing times aren't specified in official sources. Ask your local ADRC about current wait times when you call, as this varies by location and service availability.

faqs.question: What if my parent needs medical care, not just daily assistance?
faqs.answer: This program focuses on non-medical support. Your ADRC can help connect you to medical programs like STAR+PLUS if your parent needs healthcare services coordination.

programs.name: Legal Aid for Seniors through AAAs
programs.tagline: Free legal help for parents 60+ with Medicare, housing, and benefits issues through local aging offices.

localEntities.name: 28 AAAs including Concho Valley AAA (San Angelo), West Central Texas AAA (Abilene), Capital Area AAA (Austin area), Area Agency on Aging of Central Texas (Belton)

programs.intro:
If your parent is struggling with Medicare denials, nursing home complaints, or benefits applications, Texas's 28 Area Agencies on Aging provide free legal counseling and advocacy. Your parent gets one-on-one help understanding their Medicare and Medicaid rights, ombudsman support for nursing home issues, and referrals to legal aid organizations for more complex cases.

This isn't full legal representation — it's expert guidance on civil legal matters that affect seniors' ability to live independently. Because the service prioritizes those with the highest need (low income, isolation, functional impairments), your parent may receive immediate counseling or be referred to partner legal aid organizations depending on their situation.

The program covers all 254 Texas counties through regional offices, so there's always a local contact who understands your area's resources and providers.

otherRequirements: Must live in Texas
Civil legal issues only (no criminal or personal injury cases)
Parent or family member/caregiver of eligible senior

applicationGuide.summary: Call your local Area Agency on Aging directly — most provide immediate counseling or referrals during the first call.

steps.title: Find your local AAA
steps.description: Use the county directory below to find your parent's local Area Agency on Aging office and phone number.

steps.title: Call with the legal issue details
steps.description: Explain your parent's specific problem (Medicare denial, nursing home complaint, benefits issue) and have their basic information ready.

steps.title: Receive counseling or referral
steps.description: Staff will either provide immediate guidance or refer you to a partner legal aid organization based on the complexity and your parent's needs.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Immediate counseling or referral upon contact
applicationGuide.tip: Have your parent's Medicare statements, benefit letters, or complaint details ready when you call — this helps staff determine the best type of help.

contentSections.text: This program provides counseling and referrals, not direct legal representation. For full legal services, you'll be referred to legal aid organizations that have separate low-income requirements (typically 125-200% of Federal Poverty Guidelines).
Contact your local AAA first, not a statewide number. Each of the 28 regional offices knows their area's specific resources and can provide immediate help or the right referral.

faqs.question: What types of legal issues can they help with?
faqs.answer: Civil matters that affect seniors' independence: Medicare/Medicaid benefit denials, nursing home complaints, housing issues, consumer fraud, and benefits enrollment problems. They cannot help with criminal cases or personal injury lawsuits.

faqs.question: Will my parent get a lawyer, or just advice?
faqs.answer: The AAA provides expert counseling and advocacy (like ombudsman services for nursing homes), but not courtroom representation. If your parent needs a lawyer, they'll refer you to legal aid organizations that provide attorneys for qualifying low-income seniors.

faqs.question: Does my parent's income matter for getting help?
faqs.answer: No income limits for AAA services. However, they prioritize seniors with the highest need (low income, isolation, functional impairments). Higher-income seniors typically receive referrals to other resources rather than direct intensive services.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives in a rural area?
faqs.answer: All 254 Texas counties are covered by one of the 28 regional AAAs. Rural seniors often receive priority for services, and staff understand the specific challenges of accessing services in less populated areas.

faqs.question: Can I call on behalf of my parent?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members and caregivers can contact the AAA to get help for eligible seniors. Have your parent's information and details about the legal issue ready when you call.

faqs.question: How quickly can my parent get help?
faqs.answer: Most AAAs provide immediate phone counseling or referrals during your first call. For complex issues requiring attorney representation, the referral process to legal aid organizations may take additional time and separate income verification.

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.tagline: Free advocacy if your parent's nursing home or assisted living facility isn't providing proper care or respecting their rights.

localEntities.name: Local programs via ltco.texas.gov 'Find an LTC Ombudsman' tool; examples: Cizik School of Nursing (Harris County)[3], Texoma COG[4]

programs.intro:
If your parent lives in a Texas nursing facility or assisted living and you're worried about their care, safety, or treatment, the Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program provides free advocacy to investigate and resolve problems. Ombudsmen are trained advocates who protect residents' rights — from basic dignity and privacy to freedom from abuse and participation in care decisions.

This isn't a service your parent needs to qualify for based on income or assets. Any resident of a nursing facility or assisted living facility in Texas can get help, and family members of residents 60+ can also request assistance. Because ombudsmen are independent from facilities and government agencies, they can investigate complaints confidentially and work directly with facilities to resolve issues.

summary: Resident of Texas nursing facility or assisted living
otherRequirements: Must be a resident of a nursing facility or assisted living facility in Texas, or family member of resident 60+

applicationGuide.summary: Call 800-252-2412 to speak with a local ombudsman immediately — no application needed.

steps.title: Call the hotline
steps.description: Call 800-252-2412 to connect with your local LTC ombudsman. Have your parent's facility name and specific concerns ready.

steps.title: Explain the situation
steps.description: Describe the problem — poor care, safety issues, rights violations, or other concerns. The ombudsman will ask for details about your parent's situation.

steps.title: Investigation begins
steps.description: The ombudsman investigates confidentially, visiting the facility if needed and working directly with staff to resolve the issue.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Immediate response for complaints and advocacy
applicationGuide.tip: You don't need to prove wrongdoing — ombudsmen investigate concerns and can help even with general quality-of-life issues.

contentSections.text: This program only helps residents already living in nursing facilities or assisted living — it cannot help with admission decisions or paying for care.

faqs.question: What specific problems can an ombudsman help resolve?
faqs.answer: Ombudsmen investigate complaints about care quality, safety issues, abuse or neglect, medication errors, billing problems, discharge issues, and violations of residents' rights like privacy, dignity, or choice of doctor. They also help if your parent faces retaliation for filing complaints.

faqs.question: Will the facility know my parent or I made a complaint?
faqs.answer: All complaints are confidential. The ombudsman won't reveal who made the complaint unless you give permission. They investigate independently and work to resolve issues without exposing you or your parent to retaliation.

faqs.question: What if the facility doesn't cooperate with the ombudsman?
faqs.answer: Ombudsmen have legal authority to access facilities and residents. If a facility refuses to cooperate or resolve serious issues, ombudsmen can escalate to state agencies, pursue legal remedies, or represent residents in hearings.

faqs.question: Can an ombudsman help if Mom wants to move to a different facility?
faqs.answer: Yes, ombudsmen can help protect your mother's right to transfer and ensure proper discharge procedures are followed. However, they cannot help find new facilities or determine eligibility for admission — that's not part of their advocacy role.

faqs.question: How quickly will someone respond to our complaint?
faqs.answer: You'll get an immediate response when you call 800-252-2412. For urgent safety issues, ombudsmen prioritize quick action. Less urgent concerns may take longer to investigate, but someone will contact you right away to understand the situation.

faqs.question: Does this cost anything or affect Mom's benefits?
faqs.answer: The service is completely free with no income requirements. Using an ombudsman won't affect your parent's Medicaid, Medicare, or any other benefits. There are no financial eligibility requirements to meet.

stateOverview.intro: Texas offers 12 major programs to help your aging parent — 9 provide direct financial benefits or services, while 3 offer free guidance and advocacy. Financial help ranges from $100–298/month for groceries through SNAP to comprehensive medical coverage through STAR+PLUS or PACE. Free resources include one-on-one Medicare guidance and legal aid for seniors 60+. Texas uses standard federal programs alongside state-specific Medicaid waivers like STAR+PLUS and Community Based Alternatives (CBA). Most benefits require income verification, but several programs like Meals on Wheels and legal aid have no income limits. Start by checking if your parent qualifies for Medicaid cost-sharing programs — these can dramatically reduce Medicare premiums and copays.

startHere.name: Qualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB), SLMB, QI Programs
startHere.why: These programs can eliminate Medicare premiums and copays if your parent's income qualifies.

startHere.name: SNAP
startHere.why: Quick to apply and provides $100–298/month toward groceries for seniors on fixed incomes.

startHere.name: Texas SHIP
startHere.why: Free expert help navigating Medicare and applying for all cost-saving programs.

byNeed.description: All three cover comprehensive home care services but have different eligibility requirements and waitlists.
These programs can eliminate or dramatically reduce Medicare premiums, copays, and medical expenses.
SNAP provides monthly grocery money while Meals on Wheels delivers prepared meals to homebound seniors.
LIHEAP provides one-time bill payments while weatherization offers permanent home improvements to reduce energy costs.
Expert help with Medicare decisions, legal issues, and nursing home advocacy — all at no cost.
Resources and support services for family members managing their parent's care.

quickFacts: Texas Medicaid waivers like STAR+PLUS and CBA often have multi-year waitlists — apply early even if your parent doesn't need services immediately
LIHEAP in Texas provides one-time payments up to $12,300 sent directly to utility companies
Meals on Wheels has no income limits in Texas — any homebound senior can qualify
stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: Benefits like SNAP, STAR+PLUS, and LIHEAP require income verification and have specific eligibility rules — your parent must qualify financially. Resources like Texas SHIP, Legal Aid for Seniors, and the Long-Term Care Ombudsman are free to everyone regardless of income. For example, any Texas senior can get free Medicare counseling through SHIP, but only those with income below certain limits can get SNAP grocery assistance.
